title: 'Might is right' vs 'Right is might'
sub_topics: Chinese militia and coast guard swarming in WPS
freedom of navigation by US, Australia, France, UK

stances.claim: China has replaced Great Britain as the new bully in Asia — by swarming the WPS with 200+ ships and prohibiting Philippine fishing in its own EEZ, China is imposing 'Might is right' (rule of force), the same adage that Britain once forced on China through the Opium Wars and Unequal Treaties.
stances.rhetorical_move: historical reversal / poetic irony — China inflicting on others what was once inflicted on it

stances.claim: Great powers (US, Australia, France, UK) are inconsistent — they invoke parts of the Arbitral Award favorable to their national interest (freedom of navigation) but do not enforce its other parts; their assertion of 'Might is right' is selectively dressed in 'Right is might.'

If the Philippines has a strong sovereignty case over land features (Murillo-Velarde map, Treaty of Paris, Treaty of Washington), it can accept China's challenge and bring the dispute to the ICJ — under the ICJ Charter only states, not private parties, may file such suits.
stances.rhetorical_move: doctrinal escalation pathway + citation of Carpio's evidentiary work

stances.claim: The choice between 'Might is right' and 'Right is might' is the choice between rule of force and rule of law — the column ends with the open question 'Which will prevail?'
stances.rhetorical_move: binary framing closed with rhetorical question

cases: Republic of the Philippines v. People's Republic of China (2016 Arbitral Award)
laws_treaties: United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS)
events: Chinese maritime militia swarming Whitsun Reef (2021)

register_markers: binary-adage framing as title and conclusion
decision_framework_signals: applies binary 'Might is right vs Right is might' framework as the overarching lens for analyzing the WPS dispute and great-power behavior
frames historical reversals (British colonialism inflicted on China, now Chinese hegemony inflicted on Philippines) as a moral argument
uses sovereign rights vs sovereignty distinction to clarify what the Arbitral Award did and didn't decide
